commonMistakesLatex: In part (a), creating a cycle by adding an edge that connects two towns already linked in the tree.
In part (b), assuming the shortest path is simply the one with the fewest number of roads rather than the lowest total weight.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For the MST, ensure the number of edges is exactly \(n-1\), where \(n\) is the number of towns. For the shortest path, verify that no other combination of edges results in a smaller total.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Using the exponential population model \(P = 2000(1.2)^t\), evaluate the population at specific times and sketch the resulting curve.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For exponential models of the form \(P = ab^t\), the initial value is simply \(a\) (when \(t=0\), \(b^0 = 1\)). For part (b), substitute \(t=5\) and calculate. For the graph, note that since the base \(1.2 > 1\), the curve will be increasing exponentially.

steps.title: (a) Find population at t = 0
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(t = 0\) into the formula: \[P = 2000(1.2)^0 = 2000 \times 1 = 2000\] This represents the initial population (the starting value of the exponential model).

steps.title: (b) Find population at t = 5
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(t = 5\) into the formula: \[P = 2000(1.2)^5\] Calculate \(1.2^5 = 2.48832\), then \[P = 2000 \times 2.48832 = 4976.64 \approx 4977\] (rounded to nearest integer).

steps.title: (c) Sketch the exponential curve
steps.bodyLatex: Draw a smooth increasing exponential curve on the blank axes provided. The curve must pass through the two points \((0, 2000)\) and \((5, 4977)\). Since the base \(1.2 > 1\), the curve should show exponential growth (positive slope, getting steeper). Label the axes: horizontal axis as \(t\) (time in years) and vertical axis as \(P\) (population).

steps.title: Verify your points
steps.bodyLatex: Check that your graph passes through the correct coordinates. At \(t = 0\), the curve should start at \(P = 2000\) on the vertical axis. At \(t = 5\), the curve should reach approximately \(P = 4977\). The curve should be curved (not straight) and increasing.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that any number to the power of 0 equals 1, giving \(2000(1.2)^0 = 2000 \times 0 = 0\) instead of 2000.
Drawing a straight line instead of a curved exponential graph for part (c).
Not rounding correctly in part (b) — remember to round to the nearest integer as indicated.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For part (a): Verify that \(2000(1.2)^0 = 2000 \times 1 = 2000\). For part (b): Quick check — since the population grows by 20% each year, after 5 years it should be more than double the original, and 4977 is indeed more than 4000.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Calculate the actual perimeter using the scale, then convert the speed from m/min to km/h.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the dimensions from the grid to find the diagram perimeter. Apply the scale to find the real distance. Finally, convert the units carefully to match the required km/h format.

steps.title: Find diagram perimeter
steps.bodyLatex: From the diagram, the rectangle measures 8 squares by 5 squares. Since the grid is 1 cm, the dimensions are 8 cm and 5 cm. The perimeter is \(2 \times (8 + 5) = 26\) cm.

steps.title: Calculate actual distance
steps.bodyLatex: Using the scale \(1:3000\), the actual perimeter is \(26 \times 3000 = 78\,000\) cm. Convert this to meters: \(78\,000 \text{ cm} = 780\) m.

steps.title: Find speed in m/min
steps.bodyLatex: Use the formula \( \text{Speed} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} \). Kerry walks 780 m in 12 minutes, so the speed is \(\frac{780}{12} = 65\) m/min.

steps.title: Convert to km/h
steps.bodyLatex: Convert 65 m/min to km/h. Multiply by 60 (minutes in an hour) and divide by 1000 (meters in a km): \(\frac{65 \times 60}{1000} = 3.9\) km/h.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert units, especially from cm to m or minutes to hours.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: \(3.9 \text{ km/h} \times \frac{1000}{60} \approx 65\) m/min. \(65 \times 12 = 780\) m. Correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves comparing compound interest and simple interest over a one-year period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For Option A, use the compound interest formula \(A = P(1+i)^n\). For Option B, use the simple interest formula \(I = Prn\) to find the equivalent rate \(r\).

steps.title: Calculate Option A Future Value
steps.bodyLatex: The principal \(P = \$35\,000\). The annual rate is \(6\%\), so the monthly rate is \(i = \frac{0.06}{12} = 0.005\). Over 1 year, there are \(n = 12\) compounding periods. Using the formula \(A = P(1+i)^n\), we get \(A = 35\,000(1 + 0.005)^{12} \approx \$37\,158.72\).

steps.title: Determine Interest Earned
steps.bodyLatex: To find the equivalent simple interest rate, first determine the total interest earned in Option A. Subtract the principal from the future value: \(I = 37\,158.72 - 35\,000 = \$2158.72\).

steps.title: Solve for Simple Interest Rate
steps.bodyLatex: Use the simple interest formula \(I = P \times \frac{r}{100} \times t\), where \(t = 1\) year. Substitute the values: \(2158.72 = 35\,000 \times \frac{r}{100} \times 1\).

steps.title: Isolate r
steps.bodyLatex: Rearrange the equation to solve for \(r\): \(r = \frac{2158.72 \times 100}{35\,000} = \frac{215872}{35000} \approx 6.16777...\). Rounding to two decimal places, we obtain \(r \approx 6.17\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert the annual interest rate to a monthly rate in Option A.
Rounding the interest amount too early, which can lead to an inaccurate value for \(r\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: \(35\,000 \times (1 + 0.0617 \times 1) = 35\,000 \times 1.0617 = \$37\,159.50\). The slight difference is due to rounding \(r\) to two decimal places, which is expected.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves calculating annual energy consumption costs and determining the break-even point between two different appliances.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, calculate the annual energy consumption in kWh for Television B. Then, determine the annual cost difference between the two televisions. Finally, divide the initial price difference by the annual savings to find the time required to recover the cost.

steps.title: Calculate annual energy usage for Television B
steps.bodyLatex: Television B uses \(160\text{ W}\) for \(3\text{ hours/day}\). Convert watts to kilowatts by dividing by \(1000\). The daily usage is \(\frac{160}{1000} \times 3 = 0.48\text{ kWh/day}\). Over a year (\(365\text{ days}\)), the total consumption is \(0.48 \times 365 = 175.2\text{ kWh/year}\).

steps.title: Calculate annual cost for Television B
steps.bodyLatex: The electricity cost is \(25\text{c/kWh}\), which is \(\$0.25/\text{kWh}\). Multiply the annual consumption by this rate: \(175.2 \times 0.25 = \$43.80\). The difference in annual electricity cost between Television A (\(\$48.18\)) and Television B (\(\$43.80\)) is \(48.18 - 43.80 = \$4.38\).

steps.title: Determine the initial price difference
steps.bodyLatex: Television B is more expensive to buy initially. The difference in purchase price is \(\$921.90 - \$900.00 = \$21.90\). This is the extra amount the family pays upfront to purchase the more efficient Television B.

steps.title: Calculate the break-even time
steps.bodyLatex: The family saves \(\$4.38\) per year in electricity costs by choosing Television B. To find how many years it takes for these savings to cover the \(\$21.90\) price difference, divide the price difference by the annual savings: \(\frac{21.90}{4.38} = 5\text{ years}\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The difference in annual cost is \(\$4.38\). (b) It will take \(5\text{ years}\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert watts to kilowatts (dividing by 1000) before calculating the cost.
Using the wrong price difference or failing to account for the full year of usage (365 days).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: \(5 \times \$4.38 = \$21.90\), which exactly matches the difference in the purchase price of the two televisions.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves determining the equation of a linear regression line from a scatter plot and analysing how an additional data point influences the gradient.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), identify two points on the regression line to calculate the gradient \(m\) and the \(y\)-intercept \(c\). For part (b), consider the position of the new point \((10, 45)\) relative to the existing trend to determine its effect on the slope.

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: Observe the regression line on the graph. It passes through the \(y\)-axis at \((0, 2)\) and appears to pass through \((5, 18)\). Calculate the gradient \(m\) using the formula \(m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}\). Substituting the coordinates gives \(m = \frac{18 - 2}{5 - 0} = \frac{16}{5} = 3.2\).

steps.title: Determine the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Using the slope-intercept form \(y = mx + c\), we have already identified the \(y\)-intercept \(c = 2\) from the point \((0, 2)\). Substituting \(m = 3.2\) and \(c = 2\) into the equation yields the regression line \(y = 3.2x + 2\).

steps.title: Analyse the new data point
steps.bodyLatex: The new data point is \((10, 45)\). To see how it affects the gradient, compare its position to the existing line. At \(x = 10\), the original line predicts \(y = 3.2(10) + 2 = 34\). Since the actual value \(45\) is significantly higher than the predicted value \(34\), this point acts as an 'outlier' pulling the right side of the line upwards.

steps.title: Conclusion for the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: Because the new point \((10, 45)\) lies well above the current regression line, the least-squares regression line will rotate upwards to accommodate this point. This rotation increases the steepness of the line, meaning the gradient will increase.

commonMistakesLatex: Misreading the coordinates of the points on the regression line.
Confusing the effect of a point above the line with a point below the line; remember that points far from the mean in the direction of the trend exert more 'leverage' on the gradient.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the gradient calculation: \(\frac{18-2}{5} = 3.2\). The logic for part (b) follows the principle that points with high leverage (far from the mean \(x\)) pull the regression line towards them.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Solve a linear equation containing a fraction by clearing the denominator first.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the fraction \(\frac{x-1}{2}\) on the left-hand side. The key move is to multiply the entire equation by 2 to eliminate the denominator, making it a simple linear equation.

steps.title: Clear the denominator
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply both sides of the equation by 2 to remove the fraction: \[2\left(x + \frac{x-1}{2}\right) = 2 \times 9\] This gives \(2x + (x-1) = 18\).

steps.title: Expand and simplify
steps.bodyLatex: Remove the brackets on the left-hand side: \(2x + x - 1 = 18\). Combine the like terms \(2x\) and \(x\) to get \(3x - 1 = 18\).

steps.title: Isolate the variable term
steps.bodyLatex: Add 1 to both sides to isolate the term with \(x\): \[3x - 1 + 1 = 18 + 1\] This simplifies to \(3x = 19\).

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.bodyLatex: Divide both sides by 3 to find \(x\): \[x = \frac{19}{3}\] This is an improper fraction that cannot be simplified further.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply every term by 2 when clearing the denominator — only the fraction gets cleared, leaving \(x + x - 1 = 9\) which gives the wrong answer \(x = 5\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: The dividend yield is calculated by expressing the dividend per share as a percentage of the market price per share.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the dividend per share and the market price per share. The dividend yield formula is defined as \(\text{Dividend Yield} = \left( \frac{\text{Dividend per share}}{\text{Market price per share}} \right) \times 100\%\).

steps.title: Calculate the dividend per share
steps.bodyLatex: First, find the dividend earned on a single share by dividing the total dividend by the number of shares owned: \(\frac{\$810}{1500} = \$0.54\).

steps.title: Set up the dividend yield ratio
steps.bodyLatex: Now, compare the dividend per share to the market price per share. This gives the ratio: \(\frac{0.54}{27}\).

steps.title: Convert to a percentage
steps.bodyLatex: To express this ratio as a percentage, multiply by \(100\%\): \(\frac{0.54}{27} \times 100\% = 0.02 \times 100\%\).

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Complete the multiplication to find the final percentage: \(0.02 \times 100\% = 2\%\).

commonMistakesLatex: Dividing the total dividend by the total market value of the shares instead of the price per share. While this yields the same result, it is often easier to work with the per-share values to avoid large numbers.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the result by calculating \(2\%\) of the total investment. Total investment is \(1500 \times \$27 = \$40500\). Then, \(0.02 \times \$40500 = \$810\), which matches the given total dividend.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the present value of an annuity formula to determine the required monthly repayment for a loan.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the loan amount, the interest rate per period, and the total number of periods. Use the provided table to find the present value interest factor (PVIF) and divide the loan amount by this factor.

steps.title: Calculate the interest rate per period
steps.bodyLatex: The annual interest rate is \(1.5\%\), which is \(0.015\) as a decimal. Since the interest is compounded monthly, divide the annual rate by \(12\) to find the monthly rate \(r\): \[r = \frac{0.015}{12} = 0.00125\]

steps.title: Determine the total number of periods
steps.bodyLatex: The loan is for a \(30\)-year period with monthly repayments. Calculate the total number of periods \(N\) by multiplying the number of years by \(12\): \[N = 30 \times 12 = 360\]

steps.title: Find the present value interest factor
steps.bodyLatex: Locate the value in the provided table corresponding to \(r = 0.00125\) and \(N = 360\). From the table, the present value interest factor for an annuity of \(\$1\) is \(289.75411\).

steps.title: Calculate the monthly repayment
steps.bodyLatex: The loan amount \(P\) is related to the monthly payment \(M\) by the formula \(P = M \times \text{PVIF}\). Rearrange to solve for \(M\): \[M = \frac{500,000}{289.75411} \approx 1725.599...\] Rounding to the nearest cent, the monthly payment is \(\$1725.60\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the annual interest rate instead of the monthly rate.
Forgetting to convert the loan term from years to months.
Rounding the interest factor or intermediate steps too early, which leads to an inaccurate final payment.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Multiply the monthly payment by the total number of periods: \(\$1725.60 \times 360 = \$621,216\). This total includes the principal and interest, which is consistent with a long-term loan.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves using right-angled trigonometry to find side lengths and circle geometry to calculate the area of a shaded region.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify that \( riangle XYZ\) is a right-angled triangle inscribed in a semicircle, meaning the diameter \(XZ\) is the hypotenuse. Use the cosine ratio to find \(XY\), then subtract the area of the triangle from the area of the semicircle.

steps.title: Calculate the length of XY
steps.bodyLatex: In the right-angled triangle \( riangle XYZ\), we know the hypotenuse \(XZ = 16\text{ cm}\) and the angle \(\angle YXZ = 30^\circ\). Since \(XY\) is the side adjacent to the \(30^\circ\) angle, we use the cosine ratio: \(\cos 30^\circ = \frac{XY}{16}\). Rearranging gives \(XY = 16 \times \cos 30^\circ \approx 13.8564\text{ cm}\).

steps.title: Calculate the area of the semicircle
steps.bodyLatex: The diameter of the semicircle is \(16\text{ cm}\), so the radius \(r = 8\text{ cm}\). The area of a full circle is \(\pi r^2\), so the area of the semicircle is \(\frac{1}{2} \pi r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times 8^2 = 32\pi \approx 100.5310\text{ cm}^2\).

steps.title: Calculate the area of triangle XYZ
steps.bodyLatex: To find the area of the triangle, we need the height \(YZ\). Using the sine ratio, \(\sin 30^\circ = \frac{YZ}{16}\), so \(YZ = 16 \times \sin 30^\circ = 8\text{ cm}\). The area of the triangle is \(\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times XY \times YZ = \frac{1}{2} \times 13.8564 \times 8 \approx 55.4256\text{ cm}^2\).

steps.title: Calculate the shaded area
steps.bodyLatex: The shaded area is the difference between the area of the semicircle and the area of the triangle: \(\text{Area} = 100.5310 - 55.4256 = 45.1054\text{ cm}^2\). Rounding to one decimal place as requested, we get \(45.1\text{ cm}^2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong trigonometric ratio (e.g., using sine instead of cosine for the adjacent side).
Forgetting to divide the circle area by 2 for the semicircle.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which can lead to inaccuracies in the final area calculation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The area of the triangle is \(\frac{1}{2} \times 16^2 \times \sin 30^\circ \times \cos 30^\circ = 128 \times 0.5 \times 0.866 \approx 55.4\), which is consistent with the steps.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question involves using a linear regression model to make predictions and interpreting the gradient, followed by evaluating the reliability of two different models using the correlation coefficient.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a)(i), substitute the given value into the regression equation. For (a)(ii), recall that the gradient represents the rate of change of the dependent variable per unit of the independent variable. For (b), compare the strength of the linear relationships using the correlation coefficient \(r\).

steps.title: Predicting temperature
steps.bodyLatex: To find the temperature for a town \(540\text{ m}\) above sea level, substitute \(x = 540\) into the regression equation \(y = 29.2 - 0.011x\). Calculating this gives \(y = 29.2 - 0.011(540) = 29.2 - 5.94 = 23.26\). Rounding to one decimal place, we get \(23.3^{\circ}\text{C}\).

steps.title: Interpreting the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The gradient \(m = -0.011\) indicates the change in \(y\) for every \(1\) unit increase in \(x\). Since the gradient is negative, the temperature decreases as height increases. Specifically, for every \(1\text{ m}\) increase in height above sea level, the average maximum daily temperature is expected to decrease by \(0.011^{\circ}\text{C}\).

steps.title: Comparing model reliability
steps.bodyLatex: To determine which measurement is a better predictor, compare the correlation coefficients \(r\). The strength of a linear relationship is determined by how close \(|r|\) is to \(1\). For height, \(|r| = |-0.494| = 0.494\). For latitude, \(|r| = |-0.897| = 0.897\).

steps.title: Selecting the better predictor
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(0.897 > 0.494\), the relationship between latitude and temperature is stronger than the relationship between height and temperature. Therefore, latitude is the better measurement to use for predicting the town's temperature because it provides a more reliable linear model.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a)(i) \(23.3^{\circ}\text{C}\); (a)(ii) For every \(1\text{ m}\) increase in height, the temperature decreases by \(0.011^{\circ}\text{C}\); (b) Latitude is better because it has a stronger correlation (\(|r|=0.897\)) compared to height (\(|r|=0.494\)).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to round the answer to one decimal place in (a)(i).
Confusing the strength of the correlation with the sign of the correlation coefficient; remember that strength is determined by the absolute value \(|r|\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure that the interpretation of the gradient includes both the direction of change (decrease) and the specific units involved.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves solving a system of simultaneous linear equations by representing the constraints as lines on a Cartesian plane.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We are given the total number of animals as \(x + y = 31\). We need to construct a second equation based on the total number of legs, where goannas contribute \(4x\) legs and emus contribute \(2y\) legs, resulting in \(4x + 2y = 76\).

steps.title: Formulate the second equation
steps.bodyLatex: Since goannas have 4 legs and emus have 2 legs, the total number of legs is given by \(4x + 2y = 76\). To make this easier to graph, we can simplify the equation by dividing every term by 2, resulting in \(2x + y = 38\).

steps.title: Identify intercepts for graphing
steps.bodyLatex: For the first equation \(x + y = 31\), the intercepts are \((31, 0)\) and \((0, 31)\). For the second equation \(2x + y = 38\), the intercepts are \((19, 0)\) and \((0, 38)\). Plot these points on the grid and draw a straight line through each pair.

steps.title: Find the point of intersection
steps.bodyLatex: The solution to the system is the point \((x, y)\) where the two lines cross. By observing the intersection on the grid, we identify the coordinates \((7, 24)\). This means \(x = 7\) and \(y = 24\).

steps.title: Verify the solution
steps.bodyLatex: Check the values against the original constraints: \(7 + 24 = 31\) (total animals) and \(4(7) + 2(24) = 28 + 48 = 76\) (total legs). Both conditions are satisfied.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Number of goannas = 7, Number of emus = 24
commonMistakesLatex: Mixing up the coefficients for legs, such as using 2 for goannas and 4 for emus.
Failing to simplify the second equation, which can make plotting the intercepts on the grid more difficult.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the intersection point \((7, 24)\) lies exactly on both lines drawn on the grid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses a quadratic model to determine the optimal price for maximum revenue and identifies the vertical intercept of the revenue function.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To maximise revenue for a quadratic of the form \(R = ax^2 + bx + c\), we find the vertex. The vertical intercept is found by evaluating the function at \(x=0\).

steps.title: Find the vertex for part (a)
steps.bodyLatex: The revenue function is \(R = -50x^2 + 4500x + 50000\). The vertex of a parabola \(ax^2 + bx + c\) occurs at \(x = -\frac{b}{2a}\). Here, \(a = -50\) and \(b = 4500\). Substituting these values gives \(x = -\frac{4500}{2(-50)} = \frac{4500}{100} = 45\).

steps.title: Calculate the optimal price
steps.bodyLatex: The problem defines the price of the book as \((10 + x)\) dollars. Since we found the revenue is maximised when \(x = 45\), we substitute this value into the price expression: \(\text{Price} = 10 + 45 = 55\). Thus, the publisher should charge \(\$55\) per book.

steps.title: Identify the vertical intercept for part (b)
steps.bodyLatex: The vertical intercept of any function \(R(x)\) occurs where the input variable \(x = 0\). This represents the revenue when there is no price increase (i.e., the price remains at the original \(\$10\)).

steps.title: Evaluate the function at x = 0
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(x = 0\) into the given quadratic model: \(R = -50(0)^2 + 4500(0) + 50000\). This simplifies to \(R = 50000\). Therefore, the vertical intercept is \(50000\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The price to maximise revenue is \(\$55\). (b) The vertical intercept is \(50,000\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add the original price of \(\$10\) back to the value of \(x\) in part (a).
Confusing the vertex \(x\)-coordinate with the maximum revenue value itself.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For part (a), the axis of symmetry is halfway between the roots \(-10\) and \(100\), which is \(\frac{-10+100}{2} = 45\). This confirms our vertex calculation.

workedSolutions.reviewNote: The prompt references a network diagram that was not provided in the input. The steps below describe the general method for solving critical path and float time problems, which is the standard approach for this type of question.
workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves finding the minimum project completion time, identifying the critical path, and calculating the float time for a specific task.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve this, perform a forward pass to find the earliest start and finish times for each task. The longest path through the network determines the minimum completion time (the critical path). Float time is calculated as the difference between the latest finish time and the earliest finish time for a task.

steps.title: Forward Pass
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the earliest start time (EST) and earliest finish time (EFT) for each task. Start at the beginning node with time \(0\). For each task, the EFT is the EST plus the task duration. The EST of a subsequent task is the maximum of the EFTs of all preceding tasks.

steps.title: Minimum Completion Time
steps.bodyLatex: The minimum project completion time is the EFT of the final task (or the maximum EFT of all tasks leading to the finish node). In this network, this value is \(40\) minutes.

steps.title: Identifying the Critical Path
steps.bodyLatex: The critical path is the sequence of tasks where the total duration equals the minimum project completion time. Trace back from the finish node to the start node by identifying tasks where the EFT equals the EST of the following task, ensuring no slack exists.

steps.title: Calculating Float Time
steps.bodyLatex: Float time for a task is defined as \(LFT - EFT\) (or \(LST - EST\)). Perform a backward pass starting from the project completion time to find the latest finish time (LFT) for each task. Subtract the EFT from the LFT to find the float.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) 40 minutes, (b) A - C - D - E - G - J - K, (c) 1 minute.
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing earliest start time with latest start time during the backward pass.
Forgetting that the critical path must be a continuous sequence of tasks from start to finish.
Miscalculating the float time by using the wrong task duration or node values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the sum of durations along the critical path matches the total project time and that all tasks have non-negative float times.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Use the Sine Rule to find an unknown angle in a triangle, accounting for the ambiguous case where the angle is obtuse.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since we have a side-side-angle (SSA) configuration, we use the Sine Rule. Because the problem specifies that \(\angle ABC\) is obtuse, we must subtract the acute angle obtained from the inverse sine function from \(180^\circ\).

steps.title: Set up the Sine Rule
steps.bodyLatex: Identify the known pairs: side \(AC=25\) is opposite \(\angle ABC\), and side \(BC=16\) is opposite \(\angle BAC=28^\circ\). The Sine Rule states \(\frac{\sin B}{b} = \frac{\sin A}{a}\). Substituting our values, we get \(\frac{\sin \angle ABC}{25} = \frac{\sin 28^\circ}{16}\).

steps.title: Solve for the sine of the angle
steps.bodyLatex: Isolate \(\sin \angle ABC\) by multiplying both sides by \(25\). This gives \(\sin \angle ABC = \frac{25 \times \sin 28^\circ}{16}\). Calculating the right side, we find \(\sin \angle ABC \approx 0.7334\).

steps.title: Find the acute reference angle
steps.bodyLatex: Use the inverse sine function to find the acute angle: \(\angle ABC_{acute} = \arcsin(0.7334) \approx 47.18^\circ\). This is the angle that would exist if the triangle were acute.

steps.title: Determine the obtuse angle
steps.bodyLatex: Since the problem states \(\angle ABC\) is obtuse, we use the supplementary angle property \(\sin \theta = \sin(180^\circ - \theta)\). Therefore, \(\angle ABC = 180^\circ - 47.18^\circ = 132.82^\circ\). Rounding to the nearest degree, we get \(133^\circ\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the acute angle from \(180^\circ\) when the triangle is obtuse.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which can lead to an incorrect final degree.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that the sum of angles in the triangle is \(180^\circ\). With \(\angle ABC \approx 133^\circ\) and \(\angle BAC = 28^\circ\), the third angle is \(180^\circ - 133^\circ - 28^\circ = 19^\circ\). Since \(19^\circ > 0^\circ\), the triangle is valid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the properties of the standard normal distribution to calculate probabilities and expected frequencies.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), recognise that the total area under the curve to the right of the mean is \(0.5\). For part (b), standardise the raw score into a \(z\)-score using the formula \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\) and multiply the resulting probability by the sample size.

steps.title: Part (a): Probability calculation
steps.bodyLatex: The total area under the normal distribution curve to the right of the mean (\(z=0\)) is \(0.5\). The table provides the area between \(z=0\) and \(z=0.3\), which is \(0.1179\). To find the probability that a value is greater than \(0.3\), subtract this area from the total area to the right of the mean: \(P(Z > 0.3) = 0.5 - 0.1179 = 0.3821\).

steps.title: Part (b): Standardising the score
steps.bodyLatex: To compare the birth weight of \(3471\text{ g}\) to the standard normal distribution, calculate the \(z\)-score using the mean \(\mu = 3300\) and standard deviation \(\sigma = 570\). The formula is \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\). Substituting the values gives \(z = \frac{3471 - 3300}{570} = \frac{171}{570} = 0.3\).

steps.title: Part (b): Finding the probability
steps.bodyLatex: Since the \(z\)-score is \(0.3\), we use the result from part (a). The probability that a baby has a birth weight greater than \(3471\text{ g}\) is equivalent to the probability that a standard normal variable \(Z\) is greater than \(0.3\), which is \(P(Z > 0.3) = 0.3821\).

steps.title: Part (b): Calculating expected frequency
steps.bodyLatex: To find the expected number of babies out of a sample of \(1000\), multiply the total number of babies by the probability calculated in the previous step: \(1000 \times 0.3821 = 382.1\). Thus, we expect approximately \(382\) babies.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the area to the right of the mean is \(0.5\), leading to incorrect subtraction.
Using the wrong mean or standard deviation values in the \(z\)-score formula.
Rounding the final answer to a whole number too early if the question asks for an expected value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(3471\) is slightly above the mean of \(3300\), a probability of approximately \(0.38\) is reasonable, as it represents the tail end of the distribution.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the area formula for triangles and the cosine rule to solve for unknown geometric properties in a radial survey.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the area formula \(A = \frac{1}{2}ab \sin C\) to find the included angle. For part (b), determine the included angle \(D\hat{O}C\) using bearings, then apply the cosine rule to find the missing side length.

steps.title: Find angle COB
steps.bodyLatex: Using the area formula \(A = \frac{1}{2} \times OC \times OB \times \sin(C\hat{O}B)\), substitute the given values: \(466 = \frac{1}{2} \times 28 \times 35 \times \sin(C\hat{O}B)\). Simplify to \(466 = 490 \sin(C\hat{O}B)\). Then, \(\sin(C\hat{O}B) = \frac{466}{490} \approx 0.9510\). Taking the inverse sine gives \(C\hat{O}B \approx 72^\circ\).

steps.title: Calculate angle DOC
steps.bodyLatex: The bearings provided are \(150^\circ\) for \(OB\), \(330^\circ\) for \(OD\), and we found \(C\hat{O}B = 72^\circ\). The angle \(D\hat{O}C\) is the difference between the bearing of \(OD\) and the sum of the bearings of \(OB\) and \(C\hat{O}B\). Thus, \(D\hat{O}C = 330^\circ - 150^\circ - 72^\circ = 108^\circ\).

steps.title: Find length CD using the cosine rule
steps.bodyLatex: To find the length of the fence \(CD\), use the cosine rule: \(CD^2 = OD^2 + OC^2 - 2(OD)(OC) \cos(D\hat{O}C)\). Substituting the values: \(CD^2 = 31^2 + 28^2 - 2(31)(28) \cos(108^\circ)\). Calculating this gives \(CD^2 \approx 961 + 784 - 1736(-0.3090) \approx 2281.45\). Therefore, \(CD \approx \sqrt{2281.45} \approx 47.76\text{ m}\).

steps.title: Calculate total perimeter
steps.bodyLatex: The fence surrounds triangle \(DOC\), so the total length is the sum of the three sides: \(OD + OC + CD\). Using the calculated values, the perimeter is \(31 + 28 + 47.76 = 106.76\text{ m}\). Rounding to one decimal place, we get \(106.8\text{ m}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to use the inverse sine function correctly or using degrees instead of radians in a calculator.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which leads to inaccuracies in the final perimeter calculation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the triangle inequality holds for triangle \(DOC\): \(31 + 28 > 47.76\), which is \(59 > 47.76\). This confirms the side lengths are physically possible.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves calculating the future value of an annuity followed by compound interest on a lump sum.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, use the provided annuity table to find the value of the savings after 8 years of deposits. Then, treat that total as a single lump sum and apply the compound interest formula for the final 2 years.

steps.title: Calculate the value after 8 years
steps.bodyLatex: Identify the future value interest factor for an annuity of \(\$1\) at \(0.75\%\) interest over \(8\) periods from the table, which is \(8.2132\). Multiply this factor by the annual deposit of \(\$1000\) to find the total accumulated value: \(1000 \times 8.2132 = \$8213.20\).

steps.title: Apply compound interest for the final 2 years
steps.bodyLatex: The total of \(\$8213.20\) now sits in the account for another \(2\) years at a new rate of \(1.25\%\) per annum. Use the compound interest formula \(A = P(1+r)^n\), where \(P = 8213.20\), \(r = 0.0125\), and \(n = 2\).

steps.title: Perform the final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values into the formula: \(A = 8213.20 \times (1 + 0.0125)^2\). This simplifies to \(A = 8213.20 \times (1.0125)^2\).

steps.title: Determine the final amount
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the result: \(A = 8213.20 \times 1.02515625\), which equals \(8419.813...\). Rounding to the nearest cent gives the final balance of \(\$8419.81\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong interest rate from the table or the wrong number of periods.
Forgetting to change the interest rate from \(0.75\%\) to \(1.25\%\) for the final two years.
Rounding the intermediate value too early, which can lead to a slight error in the final cents.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The value should be slightly higher than the initial \(\$8213.20\) because it earns interest for two more years, which is consistent with the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Use the empirical rule to find female parameters, then apply the male scaling factors to find the height at the 84th percentile.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that 97.5% corresponds to 2 standard deviations above the mean, 16% corresponds to 1 standard deviation below the mean, and 84% corresponds to 1 standard deviation above the mean. Use the female data to find \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\), then scale these for males.

steps.title: Find female standard deviation
steps.bodyLatex: From Table 1, 175 cm is at 97.5% (\(\mu+2\sigma\)) and 160.6 cm is at 16% (\(\mu-\sigma\)). Subtracting: \(175-160.6=3\sigma\), so \(\sigma=\frac{14.4}{3}=4.8\) cm.

steps.title: Find female mean
steps.bodyLatex: Using \(\mu-\sigma=160.6\), substitute \(\sigma=4.8\): \(\mu=160.6+4.8=165.4\) cm. This gives the female distribution parameters.

steps.title: Calculate male parameters
steps.bodyLatex: From Table 2, male mean is \(1.05\mu=1.05\times165.4=173.67\) cm. Male standard deviation is \(1.1\sigma=1.1\times4.8=5.28\) cm.

steps.title: Find the male's height
steps.bodyLatex: Being taller than 84% of males means being 1 standard deviation above the mean. Height = \(173.67+5.28=178.95\) cm.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The height of the selected male is \(178.95\) cm.
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing which percentage corresponds to which z-score (e.g., thinking 84% is below the mean).
Forgetting to apply the scaling factors (1.05 and 1.1) when converting from female to male parameters.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: 175 cm is indeed \(165.4+2(4.8)=175\) cm, and 160.6 cm is \(165.4-4.8=160.6\) cm. The male height \(178.95\) cm is \(173.67+5.28\), which is 1 standard deviation above the male mean.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires identifying the key features of a linear function, specifically the gradient and the y-intercept, from its equation.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise the equation is in the form \(y=mx+c\), where \(m\) is the gradient and \(c\) is the y-intercept. By identifying these two values, you can eliminate options that do not match the line's orientation or position.

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The equation is given as \(y=-2x+2\). Comparing this to the slope-intercept form \(y=mx+c\), we see that the gradient \(m = -2\). Since \(m < 0\), the line must slope downwards from left to right.

steps.title: Identify the y-intercept
steps.bodyLatex: The constant term in the equation is \(c = 2\). This represents the y-intercept, meaning the line must cross the vertical axis at the point \((0, 2)\), which is above the origin.

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.bodyLatex: Examine the provided graphs. Look for a line that passes through the positive y-axis and has a negative slope. Any graph that slopes upwards or crosses the y-axis at or below the origin can be immediately discarded.

steps.title: Select the correct graph
steps.bodyLatex: Compare your findings with the options. Option A is the only graph that displays both a negative gradient and a positive y-intercept, matching the properties of the function \(y=-2x+2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the sign of the gradient, leading to selecting a line with a positive slope.
Misinterpreting the y-intercept as the x-intercept, which would be \(x=1\) in this case.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the x-intercept by setting \(y=0\): \(0 = -2x + 2 \implies 2x = 2 \implies x = 1\). The graph should cross the x-axis at \((1, 0)\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the sensitivity of the mean and median to the addition of an outlier or extreme value.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Calculate the original mean and median, then re-calculate them after including the new score of \(5\). Observe how the values shift.

steps.title: Calculate original statistics
steps.bodyLatex: The original dataset is \(13, 16, 17, 17, 21, 24\). With \(n=6\) scores, the median is the average of the 3rd and 4th values: \(\frac{17+17}{2} = 17\). The mean is the sum divided by \(6\): \(\frac{13+16+17+17+21+24}{6} = \frac{108}{6} = 18\).

steps.title: Update the dataset
steps.bodyLatex: Adding the score \(5\) gives the new ordered dataset: \(5, 13, 16, 17, 17, 21, 24\). There are now \(n=7\) scores.

steps.title: Calculate new statistics
steps.bodyLatex: The new median is the middle value (the 4th score) of the ordered set, which is \(17\). The new mean is the new sum divided by \(7\): \(\frac{108+5}{7} = \frac{113}{7} \approx 16.14\).

steps.title: Compare the results
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the values, the median remained \(17\), so it stayed the same. The mean changed from \(18\) to approximately \(16.14\). Therefore, the median stays the same and the mean changes.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: D. Median stays the same, mean changes.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to re-order the dataset after adding the new score.
Assuming that adding any score will change the median; always check the middle position for the new \(n\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The mean is sensitive to every value in the set, so adding any number other than the original mean will always change it. The median is resistant to extreme values, so adding a value at the end of the range often leaves the middle position unchanged.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses right-angled trigonometry to relate the height of a tower and the distance from its base using the tangent ratio.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the right-angled triangle formed by the tower height, the distance along the ground, and the line of sight. Since we know the angle of elevation and the opposite side, we use the tangent ratio to find the adjacent side.

steps.title: Identify the sides
steps.bodyLatex: In the right-angled triangle \( riangle ABT\), the tower height \(BT = h\) is the side opposite the angle of elevation \(26^\circ\). The distance \(AB\) is the side adjacent to the angle \(26^\circ\).

steps.title: Set up the trigonometric ratio
steps.bodyLatex: Using the definition of the tangent ratio, \(\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}\), we substitute our known values: \(\tan 26^\circ = \frac{h}{AB}\).

steps.title: Rearrange for AB
steps.bodyLatex: To isolate \(AB\), multiply both sides by \(AB\) and divide by \(\tan 26^\circ\), giving \(AB = \frac{h}{\tan 26^\circ}\).

steps.title: Apply the reciprocal identity
steps.bodyLatex: Recall the trigonometric identity \(\cot \theta = \frac{1}{\tan \theta}\). Substituting this into our expression, we get \(AB = h \times \frac{1}{\tan 26^\circ} = h \cot 26^\circ\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the tangent ratio with the sine or cosine ratios.
Incorrectly rearranging the formula to \(AB = h \tan 26^\circ\), which would be the result if the angle were at the top of the tower instead of the base.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \(h=10\) and \(\theta=45^\circ\), then \(\tan 45^\circ = 1\), so \(AB = 10 \cot 45^\circ = 10(1) = 10\). This matches the expectation for an isosceles right triangle.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires identifying the range of a quadratic function by considering its minimum value and end behaviour.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that \(f(x) = x^2 - 1\) is a parabola opening upwards. The range is determined by the minimum value of the function and the fact that it extends to positive infinity.

steps.title: Identify the basic property of the square
steps.bodyLatex: For any real number \(x\), the square of that number is always non-negative. Therefore, we know that \(x^2 \ge 0\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\).

steps.title: Apply the transformation
steps.bodyLatex: To find the range of \(f(x) = x^2 - 1\), subtract \(1\) from both sides of the inequality: \(x^2 - 1 \ge 0 - 1\), which simplifies to \(f(x) \ge -1\).

steps.title: Determine the vertex
steps.bodyLatex: The minimum value occurs when \(x^2 = 0\), which is at \(x = 0\). At this point, \(f(0) = 0^2 - 1 = -1\). This is the lowest point on the graph.

steps.title: State the range
steps.bodyLatex: Since the parabola opens upwards, the function takes all values from the minimum of \(-1\) up to positive infinity. In interval notation, this is written as \([-1, \infty)\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the domain (all real \(x\)) with the range (the set of possible \(y\)-values).
Selecting \((-\infty, 1]\) by misinterpreting the constant term as the maximum value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a value: if \(x=2\), \(f(2) = 2^2 - 1 = 3\), which is in the interval \([-1, \infty)\). If \(x=-3\), \(f(-3) = (-3)^2 - 1 = 8\), which is also in the interval.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires applying the quotient rule to find the derivative of a function defined as a ratio of two other functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that the gradient of the tangent at a specific point is given by the derivative evaluated at that point. Since the function is a quotient, use the quotient rule formula: \(h'(x) = \frac{f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)}{[g(x)]^2}\).

steps.title: State the quotient rule
steps.bodyLatex: The derivative of a function \(h(x) = \frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\) is given by the quotient rule: \[h'(x) = \frac{f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)}{[g(x)]^2}\]

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.bodyLatex: We need the gradient at \(x=1\), so substitute \(x=1\) into the formula: \[h'(1) = \frac{f'(1)g(1) - f(1)g'(1)}{[g(1)]^2}\]

steps.title: Calculate the derivative
steps.bodyLatex: Using the provided values \(f(1)=2\), \(f'(1)=4\), \(g(1)=8\), and \(g'(1)=12\), substitute these into the expression: \[h'(1) = \frac{(4)(8) - (2)(12)}{8^2}\]

steps.title: Simplify the result
steps.bodyLatex: Perform the arithmetic: \[h'(1) = \frac{32 - 24}{64} = \frac{8}{64} = \frac{1}{8}\]

commonMistakesLatex: Swapping the terms in the numerator of the quotient rule, which leads to a sign error.
Forgetting to square the denominator \(g(x)\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the denominator is \([g(1)]^2 = 8^2 = 64\), not just \(g(1) = 8\). The calculation \(32-24=8\) is straightforward.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires integrating a function of the form \((ax+b)^n\) using the reverse chain rule or a simple substitution.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the integrand is a power function \((2x+1)^{-2}\). Since the inner function \(2x+1\) is linear, we can use the standard power rule for integration while adjusting for the derivative of the inner function.

steps.title: Rewrite the integrand
steps.bodyLatex: Express the fraction as a power of the linear expression to make it easier to apply the power rule: \[ \int \frac{1}{(2x+1)^2} \, dx = \int (2x+1)^{-2} \, dx \]

steps.title: Apply the integration rule
steps.bodyLatex: Recall that for a linear function \(ax+b\), the integral \(\int (ax+b)^n \, dx = \frac{(ax+b)^{n+1}}{a(n+1)} + C\). Here, \(a=2\) and \(n=-2\).

steps.title: Simplify the result
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the expression by moving the negative power back to the denominator: \[ \frac{1}{-2(2x+1)} + C = -\frac{1}{2(2x+1)} + C \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to divide by the derivative of the inner function (the 2), which leads to the incorrect choice of \(-(2x+1)^{-1} + C\).
Confusing the power rule for integration with the rule for \(\int \frac{1}{x} \, dx\), which would lead to a logarithmic answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiate the answer \(f(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(2x+1)^{-1} + C\) using the chain rule: \(f'(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot (-1)(2x+1)^{-2} \cdot 2 = (2x+1)^{-2} = \frac{1}{(2x+1)^2}\). This matches the original integrand.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: The mode of a continuous random variable is the value of \(x\) that corresponds to the maximum value of the probability density function \(f(x)\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the peak of the curve on the provided graph. The mode is defined as the \(x\)-coordinate of the global maximum of the probability density function.

steps.title: Understand the definition of the mode
steps.bodyLatex: For a continuous random variable with a probability density function \(f(x)\), the mode is the value of \(x\) where the function reaches its highest point. Visually, this is the peak of the curve.

steps.title: Locate the maximum point on the graph
steps.bodyLatex: Examine the provided graph for the highest point on the curve. The coordinates of this peak are given as \(\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3}{2\pi}\right)\).

steps.title: Extract the x-coordinate
steps.bodyLatex: Since the mode is the \(x\)-value at the peak, we look at the first coordinate of the point \(\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3}{2\pi}\right)\). Therefore, the mode is \(\frac{\pi}{4}\).

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our result to the given options, \(\frac{\pi}{4}\) corresponds to option C.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the mode (the x-coordinate of the peak) with the maximum probability density value (the y-coordinate of the peak).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The mode must lie within the domain of the probability density function. Since \(\frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785\), ensure this value is consistent with the horizontal axis of the graph.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the properties of even functions and the definition of a definite integral as a signed area.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the function is even, meaning \(f(x) = f(-x)\). This implies the graph is symmetric about the \(y\)-axis. The definite integral represents the signed area between the curve and the \(x\)-axis.

steps.title: Interpret the integral
steps.bodyLatex: The definite integral \(\int_{-2}^{2} f(x) \, dx\) represents the total signed area under the curve from \(x = -2\) to \(x = 2\). Because the function is even, the area on the interval \([-2, 0]\) is identical to the area on the interval \([0, 2]\).

steps.title: Calculate the signed area on [0, 2]
steps.bodyLatex: On the interval \([0, 2]\), the region \(A\) is above the \(x\)-axis (positive area \(\frac{1}{2}\)) and region \(B\) is below the \(x\)-axis (negative area \(-\frac{3}{2}\)). The integral from \(0\) to \(2\) is \(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{3}{2} = -1\).

steps.title: Use symmetry
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(f(x)\) is an even function, the integral over the symmetric interval \([-2, 2]\) is twice the integral over the interval \([0, 2]\). Mathematically, \(\int_{-2}^{2} f(x) \, dx = 2 \int_{0}^{2} f(x) \, dx\).

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the value found in the previous step: \(\int_{-2}^{2} f(x) \, dx = 2 \times (-1) = -2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking the definite integral for the total geometric area. If you add the absolute values (\(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{2} = 2\)) and double it, you get \(4\), which is incorrect because the integral accounts for the sign of the area below the axis.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since the region below the axis is larger than the region above, the net result must be negative, which eliminates options A and B.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the complement rule and the assumption of independent events to solve for the probability of winning both games.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the probability of winning 'at least one' game is the complement of winning 'zero' games. Since the games are independent and have the same probability of success, we can model this using a binomial-style approach.

steps.title: Define the variables
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(p\) be the probability that Liam wins a single game. Since the games are independent, the probability of losing a single game is \(1-p\). The probability of losing both games is therefore \((1-p)^2\).

steps.title: Use the complement rule
steps.bodyLatex: The probability of winning at least one game is the complement of losing both games. We are given that this probability is \(80\%\), so we set up the equation \(1 - (1-p)^2 = 0.80\).

steps.title: Solve for the single game probability
steps.bodyLatex: Rearranging the equation gives \((1-p)^2 = 0.20\). Taking the square root of both sides, we find \(1-p = \sqrt{0.20} \approx 0.4472\). Thus, the probability of winning one game is \(p = 1 - 0.4472 = 0.5528\).

steps.title: Calculate the probability of winning both
steps.bodyLatex: The probability of winning both games is \(p^2\). Substituting our value for \(p\), we get \(p^2 = (0.5528)^2 \approx 0.3056\). Expressed as a percentage, this is approximately \(30.56\%\), which is closest to \(31\%\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: A. The probability is closest to \(31\%\).
commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the probability of winning one game is \(0.4\) because \(80\% / 2 = 40\%\). This ignores the fact that the events are independent and the probability of 'at least one' is not simply the sum of individual probabilities.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \(p \approx 0.553\), then the probability of losing both is \((1-0.553)^2 \approx 0.2\), and the probability of winning at least one is \(1 - 0.2 = 0.8\), which matches the given information.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires evaluating the composite function \(y=g(f(x))\) by tracking the output of \(f(x)\) as the input for \(g(x)\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Observe the range of \(f(x)\) and use it as the domain for \(g(x)\). Specifically, identify key points like the minimum and the intercepts to determine the shape of the composite graph.

steps.title: Analyse the inner function
steps.bodyLatex: Examine \(f(x)\). The graph shows that \(f(x) \ge 0\) for all \(x\), with a minimum at \((0,0)\). This means the input to the outer function \(g\) will always be non-negative.

steps.title: Evaluate at key points
steps.bodyLatex: At \(x=0\), \(f(0)=0\). Therefore, the value of the composite function is \(g(f(0)) = g(0)\). From the graph of \(g(x)\), identify the value of \(g(0)\) to locate the \(y\)-intercept of the composite graph.

steps.title: Consider symmetry
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(f(x)\) is symmetric about the \(y\)-axis (an even function), the composite function \(g(f(x))\) will also be symmetric about the \(y\)-axis. This allows you to eliminate any options that do not show this reflectional symmetry.

steps.title: Determine the end behaviour
steps.bodyLatex: As \(x \to \infty\), \(f(x) \to \infty\). Consequently, the composite function \(g(f(x))\) will behave like \(g(u)\) as \(u \to \infty\). Match the trend of \(g(x)\) for large positive values to the options provided.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the order of composition by attempting to graph \(f(g(x))\) instead of \(g(f(x))\).
Ignoring the restricted domain of the inner function's output, which limits the part of \(g(x)\) that is actually used.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that the \(y\)-intercept of your chosen graph matches \(g(f(0))\) and that the symmetry is consistent with \(f(x)\) being an even function.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires calculating cumulative frequency and cumulative percentage to interpret a Pareto chart.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find \(A\), add the current frequency to the previous cumulative frequency. To find \(B\), divide the cumulative frequency by the total number of complaints and multiply by \(100\). For the Pareto chart, identify the categories where the cumulative percentage line first reaches or exceeds \(80\%\).

steps.title: Calculate A
steps.bodyLatex: The cumulative frequency \(A\) represents the sum of all frequencies up to that row. Given the previous cumulative frequency is \(98\) and the frequency for the current row is \(62\), we calculate \(A = 98 + 62 = 160\).

steps.title: Calculate B
steps.bodyLatex: The cumulative percentage \(B\) is calculated as \(\frac{\text{cumulative frequency}}{\text{total frequency}} \times 100\). With a total of \(200\) complaints and a cumulative frequency of \(192\) for the 'damaged-item' row, we find \(B = \frac{192}{200} \times 100 = 96\).

steps.title: Interpret the Pareto chart
steps.bodyLatex: A Pareto chart displays categories in descending order of frequency. To address \(80\%\) of complaints, look at the cumulative percentage line. The categories 'stock shortage' and 'delivery fee' have cumulative percentages that reach \(80\%\) (specifically, \(50\%\) and \(80\%\) respectively).

steps.title: Identify the categories
steps.bodyLatex: Since the cumulative percentage reaches exactly \(80\%\) at the 'delivery fee' category, the manager should address the two categories that contribute to this total: 'stock shortage' and 'delivery fee'.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(A=160\), \(B=96\). (b) The manager will address 'stock shortage' and 'delivery fee'.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add the previous cumulative frequency when calculating A.
Misinterpreting the Pareto chart by looking at individual frequencies instead of the cumulative percentage line.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the cumulative percentages are strictly increasing and the final cumulative percentage is \(100\%\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the inverse variation model \(M = \frac{k}{T}\) to relate melting time to temperature.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that 'varies inversely' implies the relationship \(M = \frac{k}{T}\). Use the given data point \((T, M) = (15, 12)\) to solve for the constant of variation \(k\).

steps.title: Determine the constant k
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(M\) varies inversely with \(T\), we write the equation as \(M = \frac{k}{T}\). Substitute the known values \(T = 15\) and \(M = 12\) into the equation: \(12 = \frac{k}{15}\). Multiplying both sides by \(15\) gives \(k = 12 \times 15 = 180\).

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.bodyLatex: Now that we have found the constant \(k = 180\), substitute it back into the general form to obtain the specific equation relating the two variables: \(M = \frac{180}{T}\).

steps.title: Calculate table values
steps.bodyLatex: To complete the table for the graph, substitute the required temperatures into the equation \(M = \frac{180}{T}\). For \(T = 5\), \(M = \frac{180}{5} = 36\). For \(T = 15\), \(M = \frac{180}{15} = 12\). For \(T = 30\), \(M = \frac{180}{30} = 6\).

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.bodyLatex: Plot the points \((5, 36)\), \((15, 12)\), and \((30, 6)\) on the provided grid. Since this is an inverse variation, draw a smooth, downward-sloping curve (a hyperbola branch) passing through these points. Ensure the curve does not touch the axes, as \(T\) and \(M\) must be positive.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing inverse variation with direct variation (writing \(M = kT\) instead of \(M = \frac{k}{T}\)).
Forgetting to label the axes on the graph with the correct variables and units.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the points: \(5 \times 36 = 180\), \(15 \times 12 = 180\), and \(30 \times 6 = 180\). The product is constant, confirming the inverse relationship.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the trapezoidal rule to approximate a definite integral by dividing the area under the curve into two equal trapezoids.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the interval \([a, b] = [0, 2]\) and the number of subintervals \(n=2\). Calculate the width of each subinterval \(h\) and evaluate the function \(f(x) = \sqrt{1+x^2}\) at the required points to substitute into the trapezoidal rule formula.

steps.title: Determine subinterval width
steps.bodyLatex: The interval is \([0, 2]\) and we need \(n=2\) applications (subintervals). The width \(h\) is calculated as \(h = \frac{b-a}{n} = \frac{2-0}{2} = 1\).

steps.title: Evaluate function values
steps.bodyLatex: We need the function values at the boundaries and the midpoint: \(f(0) = \sqrt{1+0^2} = 1\), \(f(1) = \sqrt{1+1^2} = \sqrt{2}\), and \(f(2) = \sqrt{1+2^2} = \sqrt{5}\).

steps.title: Apply the trapezoidal rule formula
steps.bodyLatex: The formula for two subintervals is \(T = \frac{h}{2} [f(x_0) + 2f(x_1) + f(x_2)]\). Substituting our values gives \(T = \frac{1}{2} [1 + 2\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{5}]\).

steps.title: Calculate the final approximation
steps.bodyLatex: Using a calculator, compute \(\frac{1}{2} (1 + 2(1.4142...) + 2.2360...) \approx \frac{1}{2} (6.0644...) \approx 3.0322\). Rounding to two decimal places, we get \(3.03\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the middle term \(f(1)\) by \(2\) in the trapezoidal rule formula.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which can lead to an inaccurate final result.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The function \(f(x) = \sqrt{1+x^2}\) is always positive and increasing on \([0, 2]\). Since the trapezoids sit above the curve (concave up), the approximation should be slightly larger than the true area, which is consistent with our result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Determine the parameters of a sine function by identifying the amplitude and the period from the provided graph.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The general form is \(y=k\sin(ax)\). The parameter \(k\) represents the amplitude (the maximum vertical displacement from the equilibrium position), and the parameter \(a\) relates to the period \(T\) via the formula \(T=\frac{2\pi}{a}\).

steps.title: Identify the amplitude
steps.bodyLatex: The amplitude \(k\) is the maximum value of the function \(y=k\sin(ax)\). Looking at the graph, the peak of the wave reaches \(y=4\). Since the sine function oscillates between \(-k\) and \(k\), we identify \(k=4\).

steps.title: Determine the period
steps.bodyLatex: The period \(T\) is the horizontal distance required for the function to complete one full cycle. From the graph, one full cycle starts at \(x=0\) and returns to the equilibrium position after completing a peak and a trough at \(x=6\pi\). Thus, \(T=6\pi\).

steps.title: Relate the period to the parameter a
steps.bodyLatex: The standard period for the function \(\sin(ax)\) is given by the formula \(T=\frac{2\pi}{a}\). We substitute our observed period into this equation: \(\frac{2\pi}{a}=6\pi\).

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.bodyLatex: To isolate \(a\), divide both sides by \(2\pi\) to get \(\frac{1}{a}=3\). Taking the reciprocal of both sides yields \(a=\frac{1}{3}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the period with the frequency; remember that \(a\) is the coefficient inside the sine function, not the period itself.
Forgetting to use the formula \(T=\frac{2\pi}{a}\) and instead assuming \(a\) is simply the period value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute the values back into the function: \(y=4\sin(\frac{1}{3}x)\). If \(x=6\pi\), then \(y=4\sin(\frac{1}{3} \times 6\pi) = 4\sin(2\pi) = 0\), which matches the graph.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the Law of Total Probability to find the overall chance of an event and Bayes' Theorem to calculate conditional probability.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the two mutually exclusive scenarios: selecting a standard die or the special die. Use a tree diagram approach or the Law of Total Probability to sum the probabilities of rolling a 5 from each source.

steps.title: Calculate the total probability of rolling a 5
steps.bodyLatex: There are 3 dice in total: 2 standard and 1 special. The probability of picking a standard die is \(P(S^c) = \frac{2}{3}\), and the special die is \(P(S) = \frac{1}{3}\). A standard die has one 5, so \(P(5|S^c) = \frac{1}{6}\). The special die has three 5s, so \(P(5|S) = \frac{3}{6}\). Using the Law of Total Probability: \(P(5) = P(S^c)P(5|S^c) + P(S)P(5|S) = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{6} = \frac{5}{18}\).

steps.title: Define the conditional probability
steps.bodyLatex: We need to find \(P(S|5)\), the probability the die is special given that a 5 was rolled. By the definition of conditional probability, \(P(S|5) = \frac{P(S \cap 5)}{P(5)}\).

steps.title: Calculate the intersection probability
steps.bodyLatex: The intersection \(P(S \cap 5)\) represents the event of picking the special die AND rolling a 5. This is calculated as \(P(S) \times P(5|S) = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{6} = \frac{3}{18} = \frac{1}{6}\).

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values into the conditional probability formula: \(P(S|5) = \frac{1/6}{5/18}\). To divide fractions, multiply by the reciprocal: \(\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{18}{5} = \frac{18}{30} = \frac{3}{5}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to weight the probability of rolling a 5 by the probability of selecting that specific die type.
Confusing \(P(S|5)\) with \(P(5|S)\); always check if the condition is the 'given' part of the fraction.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since the special die is much more likely to produce a 5 than a standard die, it makes sense that the probability of it being the special die increases from the initial \(1/3\) to \(3/5\) once a 5 is observed.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Find the area enclosed between the parabola \(y=x^2\) and the line \(y=2x+3\) by integrating their difference between the intersection points.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First confirm which curve is on top by testing a point between the intersections. Since the line \(y=2x+3\) gives \(y=3\) at \(x=0\) while the parabola gives \(y=0\), the line is above. The area equals the definite integral of the upper minus lower function from \(x=-1\) to \(x=3\).

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.bodyLatex: The curves intersect at \(x=-1\) and \(x=3\). Between these x-values, the line \(y=2x+3\) is above the parabola \(y=x^2\), so the enclosed area is \(A=\int_{-1}^{3}(2x+3-x^2)\,dx\).

steps.title: Integrate
steps.bodyLatex: Find the antiderivative: \(\int(2x+3-x^2)\,dx = x^2 + 3x - \frac{x^3}{3} + C\). This gives the area as \(\left[x^2+3x-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{3}\).

steps.title: Evaluate at lower bound and subtract

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check which curve is on top and subtracting in the wrong order, giving a negative answer. Always verify by testing a point between the intersections.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The area should be positive. The region spans roughly 4 units horizontally and extends vertically about 3 units at \(x=0\), giving an area around \(4 \times 3 \times 0.5 = 6\) to \(12\); \(10.67\) is reasonable.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the sum of an arithmetic series to model the total number of cards in a structure.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the sequence as arithmetic because each row increases by a constant amount. Use the arithmetic series sum formula \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(2a + (n-1)d)\) where \(a=3\) is the first term and \(d=3\) is the common difference.

steps.title: Part (a): Summing 12 rows
steps.bodyLatex: For \(n=12\) rows, substitute \(a=3\), \(d=3\), and \(n=12\) into the sum formula: \(S_{12} = \frac{12}{2}(2(3) + (12-1)3)\). Simplify the expression inside the brackets: \(S_{12} = 6(6 + 11 \times 3) = 6(6 + 33) = 6(39)\). Calculating this gives \(234\), confirming the total number of cards.

steps.title: Part (b): Setting up the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Given a total of \(828\) cards, set the sum formula equal to \(828\): \(\frac{n}{2}(2(3) + (n-1)3) = 828\). Simplify the expression inside the parentheses: \(\frac{n}{2}(6 + 3n - 3) = 828\), which simplifies to \(\frac{n}{2}(3n + 3) = 828\).

steps.title: Solving the quadratic
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply both sides by \(2\) to clear the fraction: \(n(3n + 3) = 1656\). Divide by \(3\) to simplify: \(n(n + 1) = 552\). Expand to form the quadratic equation \(n^2 + n - 552 = 0\).

steps.title: Finding the number of rows
steps.bodyLatex: Solve the quadratic equation \(n^2 + n - 552 = 0\). Using the quadratic formula or factorisation, we find \((n - 23)(n + 24) = 0\). Since the number of rows \(n\) must be positive, we discard \(n = -24\). Thus, \(n = 23\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the common difference \(d\) by \((n-1)\) inside the formula.
Attempting to solve the quadratic equation without setting it to zero first.
Including the negative solution for \(n\), which is physically impossible for a number of rows.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the result for (b) by calculating \(S_{23} = \frac{23}{2}(6 + 22 \times 3) = \frac{23}{2}(72) = 23 \times 36 = 828\). The result is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the chain rule for differentiation and the reverse process for integration.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that the derivative of the inner function \(x^2+1\) is \(2x\), which is a constant multiple of the \(x\) term in the integral. This suggests the integral is directly related to the derivative found in part (a).

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.bodyLatex: To differentiate \(y=(x^2+1)^4\), apply the chain rule: \(\frac{dy}{dx} = n[u(x)]^{n-1} \cdot u'(x)\). Here, \(u=x^2+1\), so \(u'=2x\). Thus, \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 4(x^2+1)^3 \cdot (2x) = 8x(x^2+1)^3\).

steps.title: Relate the integral to the derivative
steps.bodyLatex: We want to find \(\int x(x^2+1)^3 \, dx\). Notice that our derivative result \(8x(x^2+1)^3\) contains the expression \(x(x^2+1)^3\). We can rewrite the derivative as \(\frac{d}{dx}[(x^2+1)^4] = 8x(x^2+1)^3\).

steps.title: Isolate the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Divide both sides of the derivative equation by \(8\) to match the integrand: \(\frac{1}{8} \frac{d}{dx}[(x^2+1)^4] = x(x^2+1)^3\). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, integrating both sides with respect to \(x\) gives \(\int x(x^2+1)^3 \, dx = \frac{1}{8}(x^2+1)^4 + C\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the constant of integration \(C\).
Forgetting to multiply by the derivative of the inner function \(2x\) when using the chain rule.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiate the final answer \(\frac{1}{8}(x^2+1)^4+C\) using the chain rule: \(\frac{1}{8} \cdot 4(x^2+1)^3 \cdot 2x = \frac{8}{8}x(x^2+1)^3 = x(x^2+1)^3\). This matches the original integrand.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Finding the translation distance \(m\) and dilation scale factor \(k\) by expressing the transformed quadratic in vertex form.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that the three transformations (right translation, vertical dilation, downward translation) produce the form \(g(x)=k(x-m)^2-5\). Then complete the square on the given quadratic to identify \(m\) and \(k\) by matching coefficients.

steps.title: Write the transformed function form
steps.bodyLatex: Starting with \(f(x)=x^2\), translating \(m\) units right gives \((x-m)^2\). Dilating vertically by factor \(k\) gives \(k(x-m)^2\). Translating 5 units down gives \(k(x-m)^2-5\). Thus the transformed function is \(g(x)=k(x-m)^2-5\).

steps.title: Factor out the coefficient
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(g(x)=3x^2-12x+7\), factor out the coefficient of \(x^2\) from the first two terms: \(3x^2-12x+7=3(x^2-4x)+7\). This isolates the quadratic expression to complete the square.

steps.title: Complete the square
steps.bodyLatex: Complete the square inside the brackets: \(x^2-4x=(x-2)^2-4\). Substituting: \(3[(x-2)^2-4]+7=3(x-2)^2-12+7=3(x-2)^2-5\). The quadratic is now in vertex form.

steps.title: Identify m and k
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing \(3(x-2)^2-5\) with \(k(x-m)^2-5\), the coefficients match: \(k=3\) and \(m=2\). Both values are positive, confirming the transformations were rightward translation and vertical dilation by 3.

workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substituting back: \(g(x)=3(x-2)^2-5=3x^2-12x+7\), which matches the given function, confirming correctness.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses an exponential growth model to determine initial values, future populations, and instantaneous rates of change.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the function \(N(t)\) as the population at time \(t\). For (a) and (b), substitute the given time values into the function. For (c), find the derivative \(\frac{dN}{dt}\) to represent the rate of change and evaluate it at the specified time.

steps.title: Find the initial population
steps.bodyLatex: The initial number of bacteria occurs at \(t=0\). Substitute \(t=0\) into the model: \(N(0) = 200e^{0.013(0)}\). Since \(e^0 = 1\), the initial population is \(N(0) = 200 \times 1 = 200\).

steps.title: Calculate population after 24 hours
steps.bodyLatex: To find the population after 24 hours, substitute \(t=24\) into the equation: \(N(24) = 200e^{0.013 \times 24}\). Calculating the exponent gives \(0.013 \times 24 = 0.312\). Thus, \(N(24) = 200e^{0.312} \approx 273.23\). Rounding to the nearest whole number, there are approximately \(273\) bacteria.

steps.title: Determine the rate of change function
steps.bodyLatex: The rate of increase is the derivative of \(N(t)\) with respect to \(t\). Using the chain rule for \(e^{kt}\), where the derivative is \(k \cdot e^{kt}\), we get \(\frac{dN}{dt} = 200 \times 0.013 \times e^{0.013t}\). This simplifies to \(\frac{dN}{dt} = 2.6e^{0.013t}\).

steps.title: Calculate the rate at 24 hours
steps.bodyLatex: Evaluate the derivative at \(t=24\): \(\frac{dN}{dt} = 2.6e^{0.013 \times 24} = 2.6e^{0.312}\). Using the previously calculated value of \(e^{0.312} \approx 1.366\), we find \(\frac{dN}{dt} \approx 2.6 \times 1.366 \approx 3.55\). The rate of increase is approximately \(3.55\) bacteria per hour.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to use the chain rule when differentiating the exponential function.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which can lead to inaccuracies in the final rate calculation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Note that \(\frac{dN}{dt} = 0.013 \times N(t)\). Checking this: \(0.013 \times 273.23 \approx 3.55\), which confirms the derivative calculation is consistent with the population value.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the compound interest formula for a lump sum and the future value of an annuity formula (via a table) to compare two investment strategies over 10 years.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For Option 1, identify the principal, periodic interest rate, and total number of compounding periods to use the compound interest formula. For Option 2, identify the quarterly payment, the number of quarters, and the quarterly interest rate to select the correct factor from the provided table.

steps.title: Calculate Option 1 future value
steps.bodyLatex: Option 1 is a lump sum investment. The monthly interest rate is \(i = \frac{0.012}{12} = 0.001\). Over 10 years, the number of compounding periods is \(n = 10 \times 12 = 120\). Using the formula \(FV = P(1+i)^n\), we get \(FV_1 = 40000(1.001)^{120} \approx \$45\,097.17\).

steps.title: Identify parameters for Option 2
steps.bodyLatex: Option 2 is an annuity. The interest is compounded quarterly, so the quarterly rate is \(i = \frac{0.024}{4} = 0.006\). The total number of quarters in 10 years is \(n = 10 \times 4 = 40\).

steps.title: Calculate Option 2 future value
steps.bodyLatex: Using the provided table for an annuity of \(\$1\) at \(0.6\%\) interest over \(40\) periods, the future value interest factor is \(45.05630\). Multiply this factor by the quarterly payment of \(\$1000\) to find the total value: \(FV_2 = 1000 \times 45.05630 = \$45\,056.30\).

steps.title: Find the difference
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract the future value of Option 2 from Option 1 to find the difference: \(\$45\,097.17 - \$45\,056.30 = \$40.87\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The value of Option 1 is \(\$45\,097.17\). (b) The difference between the two investments is \(\$40.87\).
commonMistakesLatex: Using the annual interest rate directly instead of dividing by the number of compounding periods per year.
Confusing the number of years with the number of compounding periods (e.g., using 10 instead of 120 or 40).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the interest rates are converted to decimals correctly (\(1.2\% = 0.012\)) and that the number of periods matches the compounding frequency.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: To find the global extrema on a closed interval, we compare the function values at all stationary points within the interval and at the interval endpoints.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the function is continuous on the closed interval \([-1, 7]\), the Extreme Value Theorem guarantees that the global maximum and minimum must occur at either a stationary point or one of the endpoints.

steps.title: Find the stationary points
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiate the function \(y = x^3 - 6x^2 + 8\) with respect to \(x\) to find the gradient function: \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2 - 12x\). Set the derivative to zero to locate stationary points: \(3x(x - 4) = 0\). This gives \(x = 0\) and \(x = 4\). Both values lie within the given interval \([-1, 7]\).

steps.title: Evaluate the function at critical points
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the \(y\)-values for the stationary points found in the previous step: \(y(0) = (0)^3 - 6(0)^2 + 8 = 8\) and \(y(4) = (4)^3 - 6(4)^2 + 8 = 64 - 96 + 8 = -24\).

steps.title: Evaluate the function at the endpoints
Compare all values
steps.bodyLatex: Compare the set of values \(\{8, -24, 1, 57\}\). The largest value is the global maximum and the smallest value is the global minimum.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The global maximum value is \(57\) and the global minimum value is \(-24\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check the endpoints of the interval.
Assuming the stationary points are automatically the global extrema without comparing them to the endpoint values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since the leading coefficient of the cubic is positive, the function goes to \(+\infty\) as \(x \to \infty\), so it makes sense that the maximum occurs at the rightmost endpoint \(x=7\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses a trigonometric model to determine tidal extremes, the period of the tide, and the duration for which the water depth exceeds a specific threshold.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the amplitude and vertical shift of the cosine function to find the range. Use the coefficient of \(t\) to calculate the period. Finally, solve the trigonometric equation \(d=1\) to find the time interval.

steps.title: Determine tidal extremes
steps.bodyLatex: The function is \(d=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\). The cosine function oscillates between \(-1\) and \(1\). Thus, the minimum depth occurs when \(\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=1\), giving \(d_{\min}=1.3-0.6(1)=0.7\) m. The maximum depth occurs when \(\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=-1\), giving \(d_{\max}=1.3-0.6(-1)=1.9\) m.

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.bodyLatex: The period \(T\) of a function \(y=a\cos(bt)+c\) is given by \(T=\frac{2\pi}{b}\). Here, \(b=\frac{4\pi}{25}\). Therefore, \(T=\frac{2\pi}{4\pi/25} = 2\pi \times \frac{25}{4\pi} = 12.5\) hours. This represents the time interval between two successive low tides.

steps.title: Solve for the time interval
steps.bodyLatex: Set \(d=1\) to find when the depth is exactly \(1\) metre: \(1=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\). Rearranging gives \(0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=0.3\), so \(\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=0.5\). Within one period \([0, 12.5]\), the solutions for \(\theta=\frac{4\pi}{25}t\) are \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) and \(2\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{5\pi}{3}\).

steps.title: Calculate the duration
steps.bodyLatex: Solve for \(t\) using \(\frac{4\pi}{25}t=\frac{\pi}{3} \implies t_1=\frac{25}{12}\) and \(\frac{4\pi}{25}t=\frac{5\pi}{3} \implies t_2=\frac{125}{12}\). The duration for which the depth is at least \(1\) metre is the difference between these times: \(\Delta t = \frac{125}{12}-\frac{25}{12} = \frac{100}{12} = \frac{25}{3}\) hours, which is \(8\frac{1}{3}\) hours.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) Low tide: \(0.7\) m, High tide: \(1.9\) m. (b) \(12.5\) hours. (c) \(8\frac{1}{3}\) hours.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the period formula is \(2\pi/b\), not just \(1/b\).
Confusing the range of the cosine function, leading to incorrect maximum and minimum values.
Failing to solve the trigonometric equation for both values within the period.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: At \(t=0\), \(d=1.3-0.6=0.7\) (low tide). At \(t=6.25\) (half period), \(d=1.3-0.6\cos(\pi)=1.9\) (high tide). The values are consistent with the model.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires interpreting bivariate data by analysing the correlation coefficient, the slope of the regression line, and the scatterplot context.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To provide a complete interpretation, address the strength and direction of the relationship using the correlation coefficient, explain the physical meaning of the regression line's gradient, and comment on the real-world implications shown in the scatterplot.

steps.title: Interpret the correlation coefficient
